text: The surgeon uses an accessory port for the assistant surgeon during laparoscopy. Adhesions between the splenic flexure and spleen are identified and dissected using a ligature device. Following the dissection, the left kidney is visualized, and the spleen is fully mobilized.

The surgeon identifies the Gerota's fascia overlying the kidney and adrenal gland, and dissects it to expose the left renal vein. The left renal vein is visible in the right-hand corner of the view, and the left adrenal vein is being dissected.

The surgeon clips and divides the circumferentially dissected left adrenal vein. The xerotase fascia covering the adrenal gland is dissected and removed to expose the gland, allowing for clear identification of the pheochromocytoma and facilitating a cortical sparing adrenalectomy.

After removing Girotta's fascia, the adrenal gland is fully visualized without clear demarcation of the pheochromocytoma. A laparoscopic ultrasound is used to identify the nodule, and the robot marks its location. The hook electrocautery in the robot's right arm opens the adrenal cortex at the site of the nodule.

Bleeding occurs during the procedure, managed with gauze and a suction irrigator. The surgeon identifies the nodule and performs dissection outside the pseudocapsule using bipolar cautery in the robot's left arm and hook cautery in the right arm, benefiting from the robot's stability and three-dimensional visualization.
The surgeon continues dissection around the pseudocapsule of the adrenal gland, ultimately removing the nodule. Bleeding is managed using pressure and limited electrocautery.
